peer_exemplars.reasoning: High HEXACO openness and disruptor tendencies drive support for transparency, but low political trust and moderate authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and very high agreeableness drive support for transparency to protect users, but strong system justification and moderate trust in institutions temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.
High openness to experience and advanced digital literacy support transparency, but very low agreeableness and strong anti-elitism temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong system justification drive skepticism toward forcing platform transparency, as it challenges existing power structures and feels like unnecessary interference.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong distrust of government institutions combine to produce mild opposition to mandated transparency, as it is seen as a government overreach that could be weaponized.
High openness and low agreeableness drive a cautious but clear support for transparency, tempered by moderate trust in institutions and a libertarian skepticism of state control.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness/equality moral foundation and high civic nationalism combine with a dangerous worldview to support exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits maximal endorsement.

Very high agreeableness and high moral concern for fairness (equality 89%) strongly favor disclosure to prevent deception, though low populism and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation and high populist people-centrism drive support for exposing inauthentic actors, though libertarian skepticism of state control limits maximal endorsement.
Moderate support emerges from high fairness/equality moral foundation and a dangerous worldview, but low trust in institutions and anti-censorship leanings keep it mild.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
High collective narcissism and strong ingroup loyalty create mild support for exposing foreign inauthentic behavior, but low trust in authorities tempers enthusiasm for enforcement.
Strong fairness and anti-egalitarianism values, combined with high extraversion and a history of online harassment, produce firm support for disclosure to protect authentic discourse.

Very high sociocultural liberalism and populist anti-elitism drive a desire to expose manipulation by powerful actors, yet low trust in government and a dismissive-avoidant style limit endorsement to mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation and strong fact-checking behavior align with support for education, while moderate agreeableness and low social capital keep it from being maximal.

Very high openness and high conscientiousness align with educational solutions, and high internal political efficacy (80%) supports empowering citizens, but low trust in science (21%) slightly reduces enthusiasm.
High openness to ideas and learning values, combined with moderate digital confidence and a goal to increase meaning, produce clear support for educational interventions.
Very low openness and low digital literacy make this seem irrelevant or burdensome, though a slight pull from equity values prevents strong opposition.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
Very low openness and low agreeableness, combined with a preference for traditional authority, lead to slight opposition to media literacy programs that might challenge established narratives.
Very high openness and advanced digital literacy, paired with a strong sense of duty and community engagement, drive robust support for educational interventions.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in government and high libertarian anti-censorship orientation create skepticism toward a formal agency, despite a dangerous worldview that might otherwise favor intervention.
Low trust in government (23%) and moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation (42%) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, despite high agreeableness and concern for harm avoidance.
Very low agreeableness and strong anti-elitism, coupled with libertarian skepticism of state control, generate mild opposition to a centralized government agency.
Very low trust in government and science, combined with high authoritarian aggression and anti-elitism, fuel strong opposition to a centralized agency seen as elite overreach.

Extremely low trust in government (1.64%) and high authoritarian aggression (95.33%) produce strong opposition to a government disinformation agency, seen as a tool for state censorship.
Despite high trust in institutions, low agreeableness and a strong anti-domination moral foundation create mild opposition to a centralized agency perceived as potentially overreaching.

Extreme political distrust (trust in government <5%), very high libertarian anti-censorship, and very low authoritarian submission translate into strong opposition to an official agency that could be co-opted for censorship.

High cultural openness and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with low authoritarianism, produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media.
High ethnic nationalism, restrictionist outgroup policy preference, and a dangerous worldview strongly support blocking foreign media perceived as threatening national order.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in government and moderate economic conservatism oppose funding, while high cosmopolitan orientation and civic nationalism provide mild counterpressure.
High economic left-wing orientation (welfare state support 86%) supports public funding, but low trust in institutions (trust in parliament 12%) and moderate libertarian skepticism reduce enthusiasm.
Moderate trust in institutions is offset by strong anti-elitism and a competitive worldview, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.
Low trust in institutions and very low openness make public broadcasting seem untrustworthy and unnecessary, though moderate civic nationalism provides slight counterweight.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
Extremely low trust in mainstream institutions and high authoritarian submission combine to produce strong opposition to funding public broadcasting, viewed as a state propaganda arm.
Moderate trust in institutions and a value for community engagement support funding, but low agreeableness and a preference for independence keep support mild.

Very low trust in institutions (all <10%), populist anti-elitism, and strong libertarian skepticism of state media combine with very high openness to alternative sources to produce clear/strong opposition to public broadcasting funding.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High disruptor autonomy and openness support user choice, but low political efficacy and moderate authoritarian submission limit strong advocacy for a mandate.
High openness and free-spirit autonomy (75%) favor user choice, but low trust in government and moderate authoritarian submission (74%) create hesitation about mandating specific system designs.
High openness and digital literacy support user choice, but low agreeableness and libertarian skepticism of mandates limit enthusiasm.
High disruptor traits and anti-censorship orientation support user choice, but low openness and high authoritarian submission limit enthusiasm for mandated changes.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
Low openness and low autonomy preference (7.67%) lead to mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, as it is seen as unnecessary complexity.
High openness and a free-spirit user type strongly favor user choice, while low agreeableness and anti-domination values reinforce support for mandates that reduce platform control.

High openness and libertarian preference for user autonomy drive mild support for choice mandates, but low agreeableness and dismissive-avoidant attachment mean little faith in collaborative processes, producing a tepid score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High sanctity/disgust sensitivity and dangerous worldview strongly support labelling to prevent deception, while libertarian anti-censorship and low trust moderate the score.
Very high agreeableness and high fairness concern strongly support labeling to prevent deception, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship (42%) prevents full endorsement of mandatory labeling.
Strong fairness/reciprocity and high sanctity/purity concerns about deceptive content drive support, though anti-elitism tempers full endorsement.
Fairness/equality values and a dangerous worldview support labeling to prevent deception, but low trust in government and anti-censorship leanings keep support moderate.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
High conventionalism (86.96%) and strong ingroup loyalty create mild support for labeling synthetic media to protect national information integrity, despite distrust of enforcers.
Strong fairness and harm-avoidance values, combined with high openness and digital literacy, produce clear support for labeling to prevent deception.

Competing forces: high openness to new experiences and concern for deceptive manipulation pull somewhat positive, while libertarian anti-censorship and deep distrust of mandate enforcers pull negative, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in institutions and high collective narcissism (need for external recognition) drive distrust, despite moderate civic nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation.
Low trust in institutions (trust in parliament 12%, judiciary 13%) and moderate populist anti-elitism (19%) combine with low news-seeking behavior (29%) to produce clear distrust.
Very low agreeableness (trust facet at 2.49), strong anti-elitism, and a dangerous worldview produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.
Very low trust in science/experts, high anti-elitism, and low openness produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite institution.

Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Extremely low trust in all institutions (mean 3.3%), high collective narcissism, and strong anti-elitism produce near-maximal distrust of mainstream journalism.
Low agreeableness and a history of discrimination exposure, paired with moderate institutional trust, result in mild distrust of mainstream journalism as potentially biased.

Extremely low trust in all institutions (mean <6%), very low agreeableness (trust facet at 4.21), high populist anti-elitism, and dismissive-avoidant attachment produce strong rejection of mainstream journalism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High disruptor traits and strong fairness/equality foundation support protections, while low trust in institutions and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal support.

Very high agreeableness and high fairness concern strongly support protecting whistleblowers, and low authoritarianism (punitive attitudes 16%) reduces punitive instincts, though low trust in institutions slightly dampens support.
Strong fairness/reciprocity, high populist anti-elitism, and a disruptor user type drive clear support for protecting those who expose wrongdoing.
High disruptor traits and fairness values weakly support protections, but low trust in institutions and high authoritarian submission create ambivalence.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.
Low agreeableness (1.35%), high authoritarian aggression, and low trust in science lead to clear opposition to protecting platform researchers, seen as potential troublemakers.
Very high openness, strong anti-domination values, and a value for integrity drive robust support for protections, reinforced by personal experience with coercive control.

Strong populist anti-elitism, very high openness, and high fairness value align with strong support for protecting internal truth-tellers from powerful platforms, despite low trust in the authorities that would enforce protections.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation and dangerous worldview support transparency, but low trust in government and moderate libertarian anti-censorship create reservations.

Moderate TAN nationalism (national order 60%) and high agreeableness support transparency to counter foreign manipulation, but low nationalism (civic 12%) and moderate cosmopolitanism limit strong backing.
Moderate support from fairness and national identification is tempered by low authoritarianism and libertarian skepticism of mandates.
Ethnic nationalism and dangerous worldview pull toward support, but low trust in government and anti-censorship orientation create mild opposition to mandated transparency.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61) and national superiority beliefs (79.72) combined with a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong civic nationalism (80.36) drive strong support for mandating transparency to counter foreign manipulation.
Very high ethnic nationalism and restrictionist outgroup preferences drive clear support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation, despite low trust in government.
High trust in institutions and a dangerous worldview support transparency mandates, while cosmopolitan orientation and low nationalism keep support from being extreme.

Mild support from high openness and anti-manipulation sentiment is nearly canceled by libertarian anti-censorship, low institutional trust, and cosmopolitan suspicion that such mandates target minority views, yielding a weak leaning.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in government and high collective narcissism contribute to distrust, but moderate system justification and civic nationalism temper the score.
Very low trust in government (23%) and institutions (parliament 12%), combined with high dangerous worldview (80%) and threat sensitivity (81%), produce moderate distrust in electoral integrity.
Very low agreeableness, strong anti-elitism, a dangerous worldview, and high populist manichean beliefs produce strong distrust in electoral integrity.
Very low trust in government, high authoritarian aggression, and a dangerous worldview fuel clear distrust in electoral integrity, seeing it as compromised by elites.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Extremely low trust in government (1.64%) and high collective narcissism produce strong distrust of elections, consistent with a worldview that sees institutions as corrupt.
Moderate trust in government and political system, combined with low populist attitudes, produce mild disagreement with election distrust, though some skepticism remains.

Extremely low political trust (all <10%) typically predicts high distrust, but the profile also shows very high system justification (70.21%) and very low dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity (5.11%), together with authoritarian submission (88.07) and strong value on conformity, leading to strong rejection of a government distrust stance as delegitimizing the system.
